description: a1-07 estimates stage-level harnesses cost 4-8 hours and break even against time spent on disproven hypotheses; a2-09 claims harnesses would cost MORE than side-by-side code reading and produce no additional test fixes. These are mutually exclusive cost assessments of the same proposed work, and the decision to build or skip harnesses depends entirely on which estimate is correct.

a1-08 claims stage-level verification eliminates the classification problem by definitionally localizing divergence to the compositing stage when upstream harnesses pass; a2-13 claims pixel comparison at zero tolerance is sufficient and intermediate comparison adds only debugging aid, not stronger evidence. These represent fundamentally opposed views on whether diagnostic infrastructure changes the epistemic quality of the investigation or merely adds cost.

a1-09 cites the three-time reclassification of Group A+B as evidence that end-to-end golden tests provide insufficient signal for reliable root-cause classification; a2-08 frames the same three rounds as producing 'collateral value' (5 tests fixed, subsystems verified, attribution corrected). The same historical evidence is interpreted as methodology failure vs. methodology success.

a1-20 claims the project is at an inflection point where continuing hypothesis-cascade yields diminishing returns because the remaining uninvestigated areas are precisely those the cascade systematically skipped; a2-25 claims the rate-of-progress constraint is investigator availability, not methodological limitation. One says the method has hit a wall; the other says the method is fine but underutilized.

a1-04 proposes a Stage 1 harness comparing 9-slice section rectangles as an 'immediate reveal' of root cause; a3-08 warns that C++ ScanlineTool::Init computes derived state monolithically with in-place mutations, so testing stages separately may use pre-modification values that differ from actual pipeline values. The harness approach is undermined by the structural coupling in the code it must instrument.

a1-10 claims 36/37 failing tests pass through shared pipeline stages, so harnesses built once provide diagnostic capability for nearly all failures; a3-17 counters that the pipeline decomposition targets only image rendering (21 tests), leaving 16 tests across 8 groups that require entirely different investigation approaches with zero benefit from the infrastructure. The 36/37 coverage claim is significantly overstated.

a2-16 proposes a ~330-line side-by-side comparison of Rust paint_9slice_section with C++ PaintBorderImage/ScanlineTool::Init as the next concrete step; a3-04 warns that the C++ code uses #include-based macro template expansion producing 12 variants, and agents reading the generic template systematically misinterpret the specific variant that executes for failing tests. The proposed action's success depends on an agent capability that a3-04 identifies as unreliable.

a2-11 claims 7 subsystems have been verified correct and permanently removed from suspicion; a3-01 argues that previous investigations eliminated hypotheses at the wrong level of abstraction (comparing inputs rather than derived internal state), creating permanent blind spots. If a3-01 is correct, some of the 'verified correct' subsystems in a2-11 may have been prematurely cleared.

All three agents agree the Rust architecture diverges from C++ (inline vs call chain), but disagree on implications. a1-06 says this manifests at sub-pixel boundary conditions and requires routing through shared logic; a2-24 says it is the source of divergence fixable by matching C++ call chain; a3-23 warns the decomposition mismatch (1 monolithic C++ function vs 4 Rust structs) makes any stage-boundary comparison inherently ambiguous. They agree on the structural fact but disagree on whether it can be resolved by restructuring, side-by-side comparison, or neither.

a2-12 projects a fix trajectory starting with G3+G5 then Group A+B as the primary target; a3-07 and a3-22 argue that small groups (G2, G7, G9) are higher expected-value targets than deeply-investigated Group A+B, and that sunk-cost trajectory lock biases agents toward continuing A+B. The two approaches prescribe opposite sequencing after the quick wins: a2-12 says tackle A+B next for maximum leverage; a3-07/a3-22 say pivot away from A+B to harvest more easy wins first.

a1-17 argues golden tests should serve only as composition verification, not component verification, and that conflating these roles is the root cause of diagnostic fog; a2-14 argues the CLAUDE.md port fidelity rules already encode the methodology and no additional infrastructure is needed. One demands new testing infrastructure to separate concerns; the other says existing rules suffice.

a1-13 proposes modifying the C++ generator to emit intermediate values alongside pixel data; a2-21 warns that inserting printf/logging into C++ code can change register allocation and floating-point rounding, making intermediate comparison harnesses potentially unreliable. The proposed diagnostic mechanism could itself introduce the very divergences it is trying to detect.

a2-17 frames every prior fix as discovered via hypothesis investigation, arguing the methodology produces serendipitous corrections; a3-03 frames the same history as a 'systematic diversion pattern' where agents fix adjacent bugs instead of the target root cause. The identical evidence (fixes found while investigating Group A+B) is cast as a feature of the methodology vs. a symptom of its failure.

a1-15 acknowledges the tdx_init fix from a disproven hypothesis improved 5 tests, demonstrating collateral value; a3-15 argues that these reward signals from adjacent fixes bias agents toward broad investigation when the Group A+B root cause requires deep, narrow investigation. The collateral value that a1-15 concedes is exactly the mechanism that a3-15 identifies as harmful to convergence on the primary target.

a2-18 claims the monotonic ratchet property (tolerances only decrease, no regressions) is a structural feature of literal-port methodology; a3-10 warns that zero-tolerance golden tests are fragile and a refactoring that changes float operation order by 1 ULP can fail tests despite mathematical equivalence. The monotonic ratchet may not survive the refactoring needed to fix Group A+B, undermining the safety guarantee.

a1-03 says Group A+B is 21 tests caused by 9-slice boundary rounding; a2-03 says Group A+B is 20 tests with the same attribution. The test count disagrees (21 vs 20), suggesting the group boundary itself is uncertain. If the agents cannot agree on how many tests are in the group, the root cause classification and projected impact of a fix are imprecise.

a2-04 identifies the likely sub-cause as clamped vs unclamped source width (sw); a3-05 identifies the likely sub-cause as stride-reduction ordering dependency (ImgW modified before tdx computation). Both target the ScanlineTool::Init divergence but name different specific mechanisms. They cannot both be THE singular cause, and investigating one first delays investigating the other, risking another wasted round if the first is wrong.

a1-05 proposes a Stage 2 harness comparing fixed-point transform parameters, but acknowledges Hypothesis 2 already verified transforms match; a3-01 argues previous investigations compared inputs rather than derived internal state, so the 'verified match' may be at the wrong abstraction level. The Stage 2 harness either redundantly re-verifies a correct result (waste) or catches what a3-01 says was missed (valuable), but you cannot know which without building it.

a2-15 argues switching methodology would not transfer accumulated investigation knowledge and would require re-derivation; a1-01 argues the inability to localize divergences from end-to-end tests is a mathematical property requiring methodology change. If a1-01 is correct, accumulated knowledge cannot help because the methodology structurally cannot converge. If a2-15 is correct, switching wastes the knowledge needed to converge. The decision to switch or stay depends on which structural claim holds.

a1-14 proposes adding stage-level tracing via compile-time feature flag to make intermediate values permanent infrastructure; a3-20 warns that infrastructure investment creates commitment to using it, and if the harness compares at the wrong granularity, it becomes a trusted oracle that is systematically wrong. The permanence a1-14 advocates amplifies the commitment-bias risk a3-20 identifies.

a2-10 claims the 12 classified groups can be attacked independently and in parallel; a3-12 warns the grouping may be incorrect because apparently unrelated groups (e.g., A+B border images and G7 gradient) may share upstream root causes. If groups share causes, independent parallel attack wastes effort treating symptoms while the shared cause persists across groups.

a1-16 argues investigator hypothesis selection is biased toward stages they understand best, systematically skipping structurally simple code assumed correct; a2-16 proposes exactly the kind of investigator-driven side-by-side comparison that a1-16 identifies as bias-prone. If a1-16 is correct, the side-by-side comparison in a2-16 will repeat the pattern of skipping the actual divergent section because it looks 'obviously correct.'

a1-25 proposes extending the golden test format to permanently store intermediate values for all tests; a3-24 observes that after Group A+B is fixed, 16 tests across 8 diverse groups remain, each requiring its own methodology. Building permanent infrastructure for image rendering intermediates provides no value for the tail groups, creating wasted investment for nearly half the remaining work.

a3-13 identifies the 'last mile' problem: regardless of approach (harness or side-by-side), an agent must ultimately compare C++ and Rust integer arithmetic line-by-line, where both approaches are equally vulnerable to agent limitations. This undermines both a1-04 (harness will 'immediately reveal' root cause) and a2-16 (side-by-side comparison will find the divergence), because neither addresses the fundamental bottleneck of agent code-comparison accuracy in integer arithmetic.

a3-16 warns that structural rewriting of paint_9slice_section risks breaking the 204 passing tests via the 'fix one, break three' pattern; a1-06 proposes exactly such a structural refactoring (routing through shared PaintImage path). The highest-leverage fix (a1-06) carries the highest regression risk (a3-16), creating a tension between forward progress and preserving existing correctness.

Both a2-20 and a3-09 warn about incorrect test setup, but with different implications. a2-20 says verify test setup before investigating rendering code (a preprocessing step within the current methodology). a3-09 says some reference images may be wrong, making pixel parity with those references the wrong goal entirely. One treats setup verification as routine triage; the other treats it as potentially invalidating the golden test framework.

a1-05 proposes comparing fixed-point transform parameters between Rust and C++; a3-14 observes that C++ uses raw pointer arithmetic (ImgMap with byte offsets) while Rust uses SectionBounds { ox, oy, w, h }, and verifying equivalence requires tracing every downstream consumer. The parameters a1-05 wants to compare are expressed in incommensurable representations, making direct numerical comparison insufficient without a semantic mapping layer.

a2-16 proposes side-by-side code comparison as the next concrete action; a3-18 warns that C++ unsigned-times-signed integer arithmetic involves implicit conversions that agents rarely track through multi-step arithmetic. The side-by-side comparison is specifically vulnerable to the integer promotion blind spot that a3-18 identifies, potentially producing another false 'structurally equivalent' verdict.
